preamble: The shift from the voiceless dental fricative [θ] to the voiceless stop [t] is known as stopping. It is a very common dialectal variation, particularly in languages that lack dental fricatives in their core phonology.

examples.note: Dental stopping is a defining feature of Hiberno-English.
Many Indian languages lack dental fricatives, leading to stopping.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
